title: Mortgage Underwriting AI: 80% Inference Cost Reduction
subtitle: Systematic model right-sizing replaced Claude/Sonnet with task-specific small models and intelligent routing
description: A mortgage underwriting platform was using Claude and Sonnet for virtually every step in its document processing pipeline  20-40+ documents per loan application. Costs were high and growing. Worse, format failures in LLM responses were breaking automated workflows frequently enough to require daily manual intervention. We audited every inference call, replaced ~60% of them with encoder models, task-specific fine-tuned models, and deterministic code, and routed the remaining frontier model calls to a last-resort layer. Result: 80% cost reduction, near-zero format failures, faster processing, and sensitive applicant data now staying within the client's own infrastructure for most operations.

priorExperience.note: Delivered through an engagement at a prior organization

portfolio.projectSummary:
The client ran a mortgage underwriting system where Claude and Sonnet handled nearly every step: document classification, entity extraction, cross-referencing income and identity records, and compliance checks. With 20-40+ documents per loan application, API costs were high and growing. The deeper problem was reliability  despite ontology constraints and few-shot prompting, the LLMs would periodically return responses in the wrong format, breaking the downstream automation. Fixing those failures had become a daily manual operation.

We started with a systematic audit of every inference call in the pipeline: what was the model actually being asked to do, and did it genuinely require a frontier decoder? Government documents  W-2s, bank statements, property deeds, identity cards  follow predictable, structured formats. Classification tasks (is this a W-2 or a bank statement?) didn't need Claude at all; a fine-tuned encoder-based model achieved higher accuracy at a fraction of the cost and returned a label, not free text, eliminating format ambiguity entirely. Document splitting was pure deterministic code. Entity extraction from structured government docs was handled by task-specific small generative models. Around 60% of all inference calls turned out to be replaceable with no quality loss.

The remaining frontier model calls were kept as a last-resort routing layer for genuinely complex cross-referencing and edge-case reasoning. The result was a tiered intelligent routing system: encoder classifiers for document typing, small fine-tuned models for structured extraction, code for deterministic operations, and Claude/Sonnet only when truly necessary. Costs dropped 80%. Format failures dropped to near-zero, eliminating the daily manual repair burden. Processing speed increased substantially because encoder and small models respond in milliseconds. And since most sensitive applicant documents  identity cards, SSNs, financial records  are now processed locally rather than sent to a cloud API, the client's compliance posture improved significantly.

challenges.title: Format Reliability at Scale
challenges.description: Despite ontology validation and few-shot prompting, Claude/Sonnet would periodically return responses in unexpected formats  enough to break automated workflow steps on a daily basis. The root cause was using decoder-based generative models for tasks that are structurally classification or structured extraction problems. Replacing those calls with encoder-based models and fine-tuned small generative models eliminated format ambiguity: the output is a label or a fixed schema, not open-ended text. Format failures dropped to near-zero.

challenges.title: Identifying What Actually Needs an LLM
challenges.description: The hardest part of the engagement wasn't the engineering  it was the evaluation. The client had implicitly treated all document AI as too complex for deterministic approaches. We built a task-by-task evaluation protocol, running each pipeline step against 500+ representative samples and comparing LLM output quality against rule-based and smaller-model alternatives. The evaluation revealed that roughly 60% of inference calls were replaceable with no measurable quality loss  a finding that unlocked the 80% cost reduction and couldn't have been discovered without rigorous measurement.

portfolio.outcome: Monthly AI infrastructure costs reduced 80%. Daily manual workflow repair dropped to a rare edge case. Loan application processing became significantly faster with encoder and task-specific small models handling the heavy lifting. Sensitive applicant data  identity documents, Social Security numbers, financial records  now stays within the client's infrastructure for the vast majority of operations, substantially simplifying their data privacy and compliance obligations.

useCases.title: Systematic Cost Audit
useCases.description: Before optimizing, we mapped every LLM call to the complexity of its actual task. Around 60% were classification or structured extraction jobs that didn't need a frontier decoder at all  they needed the right tool for the job.

useCases.title: Intelligent Model Routing
useCases.description: Encoder models for document typing, fine-tuned small models for entity extraction, deterministic code for structured fields  with Claude/Sonnet as a last resort only. Each step matched to the cheapest capable tool.
